title: The Kastelholm Witch Trials (1665–1668)
summary: The Kastelholm witch trials were the largest witch trials in Finnish history, held at Kastelholm Castle in Åland between 1665 and 1668. Governor Nils Psilander, educated at the University of Dorpat (Tartu) where witchcraft demonology was a major subject, conducted the trials. Unlike typical Finnish witch trials which involved single male defendants accused of sorcery, the Kastelholm trials were a mass trial modeled on continental European patterns. Several women were accused of attending a Witches' Sabbath and making a pact with the Devil. Six women were ultimately executed. The trials were unique in Finland for their scale and their focus on diabolical witchcraft rather than traditional folk magic. The first woman executed was Karin Persdotter, and the events have since inspired the 2016 Finnish film 'Devil's Bride' (Tulen morsian).

title: King Erik XIV's Imprisonment at Kastelholm
summary: In the autumn of 1571, the deposed Swedish King Erik XIV was imprisoned at Kastelholm Castle by his brother Johan III. Erik had been dethroned in 1568 and was moved between various castles before being held on Åland. Local legend holds that Erik's ghost still wanders the halls of Kastelholm, his footsteps echoing through the stone corridors at night. The imprisonment at Kastelholm is part of a larger body of historical folklore surrounding the castle, which first appears in written records in 1388. The castle has witnessed sieges, royal visits, witch trials, and fires — and each layer of its history has contributed to its reputation as one of the most haunted places in Åland. During the restoration of the castle in the 20th century, workers reported unexplained sounds and cold drafts, which some attribute to the restless spirits of its many historical occupants.

title: The Kachinas Bring Agriculture
summary: In Hopi tradition, during a great drought when the people were starving, they heard singing and dancing coming from the San Francisco Peaks (Nuvatukya'ovi). Investigating, they met the Kachinas — ancestral spirits and supernatural beings who inhabit the peaks. The Kachinas returned with the Hopi to their villages and taught them agriculture, including how to plant and care for corn, beans, and squash. They also taught the Hopi their ceremonial dances and songs, which are vital for bringing rain to the arid land. For six months each year (from winter solstice to July), the Kachina spirits leave the San Francisco Peaks and live in the Hopi villages. Men who put on the masks become the Kachinas — the mask is not a costume but a face that transforms the wearer. The Niman (Homegoing) ceremony in late July marks the Kachinas' return to the mountains.
source: Wikipedia: Hopi mythology; Pecina and Pecina, Hopi Kachinas (2013)

title: The Kana-a Kachinas of Sunset Crater
summary: At old Mishongnovi lived a young maiden who refused all suitors. A Kachina youth from the San Francisco Peaks courted her through magical means — traveling by rainbow — and brought her across the desert to Palotsmo (Sunset Crater), home of the Kana-a Kachinas. There she underwent tests in the kiva, surviving four nights in an icy room with the North Wind, and passed. She married the Kachina youth. But a wicked man impersonated the Kachina husband and deceived the maiden. The Kana-a Kachinas at Sunset Crater took pity on the people suffering from the consequences of this deception. They brought sweet corn and food to the Hopi villages, dancing through the streets, asking each family to leave an ear of corn in their empty storage room. When the people awoke the next morning, their storage rooms were miraculously full of corn. The Kana-a Kachinas then entered a small butte near Mishongnovi, which remains their home.
source: Edmund Nequatewa, Truth of a Hopi; Sacred-texts.com

title: The Kachina Spirits of the Pueblo
summary: Across Pueblo cultures, the Kachinas (Katsinam) are supernatural beings who are the spirits of ancestors, natural forces, and all things in the universe — rocks, stars, animals, and plants. They reside in the San Francisco Peaks (north of Flagstaff, Arizona) and come to the pueblos for six months each year during the winter season. The Kachinas bring rain, fertility, and other blessings through ceremonial dances. Each Kachina has a specific identity, mask, songs, and role in the ceremonial calendar: Crow Mother brings rain, the warrior Kachinas police behavior, and the clown Kachinas expose human pretension through comedy. When a man wears the Kachina mask, he becomes the spirit, not merely a representation. The tihu (Kachina dolls) given to children are teaching objects to help them learn to recognize each Kachina. The Kachinas emerged from the underworld with the people and taught them how to live properly.
source: Various Pueblo oral traditions; Wikipedia: Kachina; Susanne and Jake Page publications

title: The Kachina Tradition (Zuni)
summary: In Zuni tradition, the Kachinas (Kâkâ) originated from the search for itiwana, the Middle Place. The priest Ka'wimosa sent his sons in different directions to find the center of the world. Kiaklo went north, the Ánahoho áchi went south, and Síweluhsiwa and Síwiluhsitsa went east, where they had ten children from an incestuous union — the first Koyemshi (Mudheads), sacred clowns who speak idocies yet utter prophecies. Meanwhile, the Zuni divided into groups led by the Beloved Twins. Children who fell into the river during a crossing became lizards, frogs, turtles, and newts — the lost Little Ones who learned the Dance of Good (Kókokshi) from the god priests of Kóthluwalawan. Duck brought Kiaklo to the council hall where he learned the story of creation and the sacred dances. He returned to teach the Zuni the Kachina ceremonies, the masks, costumes, and rituals that connect the living with the spirits and the ancestors. The masks are considered so sacred that they guarantee the owner's admission to the dance house of the gods after death.
source: Frank Cushing, Outlines of Zuni Creation Myths (1896); Ruth Bunzel, Introduction to Zuni Ceremonialism

title: Kishelamakank's Dream of Creation
summary: In the beginning, before there was anything, there existed only an endless void of darkness. Within this emptiness dwelled Kishelamakank (He Who Creates Us By Thought), the Great Spirit. While in the void, the Creator fell into a deep sleep and dreamed of the world as it would become — mountains, forests, animals, and people. Upon awakening, He brought His dream into reality. He created helper spirits: the Grandfathers of the East, North, and West, and the Grandmother of the South. Together, they shaped the earth. From a sacred tree, the first man emerged from the roots. When the tree bent and touched the ground, the first woman sprang forth. This story affirms the Lenape understanding that all creation began not with violence but with a divine dream.
source: Brinton, Daniel G. - The Lenape and Their Legends (Gutenberg); ordoluporumpma.org; native-languages.org

title: Kanati and Selu: The Origin of Game and Corn (Cherokee)
summary: Kanati (The Lucky Hunter) and Selu (Corn) were the first Cherokee man and woman. Kanati would hunt by moving a rock at the entrance of a cave, releasing an animal and killing it. Their son befriended a Wild Boy born from the blood of slaughtered game. The boys secretly followed Kanati and discovered the cave; they opened it, allowing all animals to escape, which is why humans must hunt for food today. Selu would produce corn by rubbing her stomach, and beans by rubbing her sides. When the boys discovered her secret, Selu knew she would die. She instructed them to drag her body seven times around a cleared circle, then seven times inside it, and corn would grow by morning. The boys did not complete the ritual properly, which is why corn only grows in certain places. The story establishes the Cherokee relationship with hunting, agriculture, and the origin of food scarcity.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; Mooney, Myths of the Cherokee

title: Kashehotapalo (Choctaw)
summary: Kashehotapalo is a creature from Choctaw mythology that is neither man nor beast. It has the legs and hooves of a deer but the body of a man. Its head and face are small and shriveled, and it is said that anyone who looks at it will be visited by evil. Kashehotapalo delights in frightening hunters who venture near the swamps it inhabits. It sneaks up behind them and lets out a loud call that sounds like a woman's scream before quickly fleeing into the darkness. Though it does not physically harm people, it takes great pleasure in their terror. Its name comes from 'kasheho' (woman) and 'tapalo' (call). The creature serves as a guardian of the wild places, warning against entering certain swamps and forests.

title: Kwanokasha: The Spirit Who Grants Powers (Choctaw)
summary: Kwanokasha is a powerful Choctaw spirit associated with caves and rock shelters, who appears to young children during vision quests. According to legend, Kwanokasha takes children who wander into certain caves into the spirit world, where they are tested. The spirit offers them three choices: a knife (representing the power to heal through surgery), herbs (representing the power to heal through medicine), or a pebble (representing the power to heal through prayer and ritual). The child's choice determines what kind of medicine person they will become. Those who choose nothing may be cursed with sickness or bad luck. The Kwanokasha legend underscores the Choctaw belief in the sacred nature of caves as gateways to the spirit world and the importance of choosing one's path wisely.
source: native-languages.org Choctaw Legends; Choctaw oral tradition

title: Kolowa: The Hairy Ogre (Creek)
summary: Kolowa is a fearsome, hairy, man-eating ogre from Muscogee Creek mythology. Described as being covered in thick hair and possessing enormous strength, Kolowa lives in the deep forest and preys on humans who stray too far from their villages. Some modern Creek storytellers translate the name as 'gorilla.' Kolowa is particularly dangerous because it can imitate human voices to lure victims into the woods. Parents told stories of Kolowa to keep children from wandering off alone. The creature is one of several 'Man-Eater' figures in Creek mythology, along with Isti Papa, the Great Lion. Warriors and hunters who confronted Kolowa had to rely on their courage, cleverness, and spiritual protection to survive the encounter.

title: Kaats' and the Bear Woman (Tlingit)
summary: The Tlingit hunter Kaats' was hunting in the mountains near Glacier Bay when he encountered a bear who removed her skin and became a beautiful woman. He married her, and they had children who were half-human, half-bear. His bear wife provided for him and taught him about the forest, but Kaats' eventually grew homesick for his human village. When he tried to leave with their children, the bear wife pursued him. She called upon her bear relatives, and a great confrontation ensued. Kaats' escaped with the help of his human relatives, but the bear wife reclaimed her children and returned to the forest. The descendants of these children became the Bear Clan among the Tlingit. This story explains clan origins and the special relationship between the Tlingit Bear Clan and bears, who are considered ancestral relatives rather than mere animals. It also teaches profound lessons about cross-cultural marriage, identity, and belonging.
source: Tlingit oral tradition; Alaska Native Knowledge Network

title: Komokwa the Undersea Chief (Kwakwaka'wakw)
summary: Komokwa (also known as Qomoqua or Copper-Maker) is the powerful chief of the undersea world in Kwakwaka'wakw mythology. He lives in a magnificent copper house beneath the ocean, where he controls all the wealth of the sea, including seals, sea lions, salmon, and the precious copper that gives him his name. Komokwa appears as a figure with a large, bulbous head covered in feathers or hair, often depicted with a wide mouth and protruding tongue. He sometimes takes the form of a sea monster or appears riding a Sisiutl canoe. Humans who visit his underwater kingdom must undergo dangerous trials. In some stories, a person who drowns is taken to Komokwa's house and may return with great wealth and supernatural power. Komokwa is both feared and respected; he is a generous giver of wealth to those who treat him properly but also a dangerous being who can drown anyone who disrespects the sea.
source: Franz Boas, Kwakiutl Ethnography; Kwakwaka'wakw oral tradition

title: Kwatyat and Tihtipihin Rescue Their Mother (Nuu-chah-nulth)
summary: Two Nuu-chah-nulth brothers, Tihtipihin and Kwatyat, witnessed a great monster swallow their mother whole. Without hesitation, they willingly allowed themselves to be swallowed by the same monster so they could rescue her. Inside the monster's stomach, they found their mother alive along with other swallowed victims. Using their wits and supernatural strength, the brothers cut through the monster's stomach from the inside, freeing themselves, their mother, and all the other captives. They escaped safely as the monster perished. This story of filial devotion and brotherly courage is one of the most important Nuu-chah-nulth hero tales. Kwatyat, the younger brother, is particularly significant as he is recognized as a transformer figure in Nuu-chah-nulth mythology who later helped shape the world. The story emphasizes family loyalty, bravery in the face of overwhelming danger, and the power of cooperation between siblings.

title: Komokwa and the Undersea Kingdom (Haida)
summary: In Haida tradition, the undersea world is ruled by powerful supernatural beings who control the wealth of the sea. The sea chiefs live in houses made of copper and abalone shell, surrounded by supernatural beings including the killer whale people, seal people, and halibut people. Those who drown do not truly die but are taken to live in the undersea kingdom, where they may acquire great supernatural power and wealth. The supernatural killer whales (SGaana) are the most powerful of the undersea beings; they can transform into human form and own houses, territories, and crests. The Haida people take their clan crests from these supernatural beings, copying their likenesses for use on totem poles, blankets, and ceremonial regalia. The relationship between the Haida and the undersea kingdom is reciprocal: humans show respect through proper protocols, and the supernatural beings provide salmon, halibut, seals, and whales for the people's survival.
source: Haida oral tradition; Haida Gwaii Pledge; Terri-Lynn Williams-Davidson

title: Kwatyat the Transformer (Nuu-chah-nulth)
summary: Kwatyat is one of the most important transformer figures in Nuu-chah-nulth mythology. After his adventures with his brother Tihtipihin rescuing their mother from the monster, Kwatyat traveled the land transforming the world and making it ready for human beings. He changed dangerous beings into harmless animals, taught people how to fish and hunt, and established the proper relationship between humans and the supernatural world. Kwatyat also had a trickster aspect — he could be foolish and greedy, and many stories tell of his failed attempts to imitate others or his schemes backfiring. In one story, he tried to catch salmon by building a weir but was too impatient and it collapsed. In another, he tried to fly like an eagle and fell into the ocean. Despite his flaws, Kwatyat's transformations established the fundamental order of Nuu-chah-nulth life. He is sometimes paired with or compared to Raven, sharing the role of culture hero and trickster in a complementary way.

title: Kalala Ilunga: The Founder of the Luba Empire
summary: Kalala Ilunga, son of Mbidi Kiluwe and Bulanda, was born with the destiny of a conqueror. From childhood, he showed extraordinary strength, intelligence, and mystical gifts far beyond his years — he was said to praise himself for future exploits on the very day of his birth. His uncle, King Nkongolo, grew jealous and afraid of the young prince's growing power. Nkongolo plotted to kill Kalala by digging a pit filled with spears and fetishes in the dance hall, hoping Kalala would fall in during a ceremonial dance. But the Mbudye priests warned Kalala, and he danced carefully, avoiding the trap. When Nkongolo's warriors attacked, Kalala defeated them and pursued Nkongolo. He caught the tyrant, beheaded him, and returned to the capital with his head and genitals as proof of victory. The people proclaimed him Mulopwe (Emperor). Kalala Ilunga expanded the Luba kingdom into an empire, establishing a sacred dynasty that would rule for centuries.
source: Luba oral tradition, Wikipedia, Mbudye historians
moral: Destiny cannot be stopped by the plots of the wicked.

title: Kintu and Nambi: The First People of Buganda
summary: In the beginning, Kintu was the only man on earth, wandering the plains of Uganda alone with only a cow for companionship. One day, Nambi and her younger sister came down from the sky on a rainbow. Nambi saw Kintu and was curious about this strange creature she had never seen before. She fell in love with him and wanted to take him to meet her father, Ggulu (the Sky God). But Nambi's brother Walumbe, a jealous and destructive spirit, opposed the union. Kintu was subjected to five impossible trials by Ggulu — he had to eat a houseful of food in one night, chop an entire forest, and retrieve dew in a leaky pot. With Nambi's secret help, he passed them all. Ggulu gave his blessing, and Kintu and Nambi prepared to descend to earth. But Ggulu warned them: 'Do not return to the sky for anything forgotten.' Nambi had forgotten millet for her chickens and sent Kintu back — and Walumbe insisted on joining them, bringing death to humanity forever.
source: Ganda oral tradition, Buganda kingdom folklore
moral: Small mistakes in haste can have enormous and permanent consequences.

source_references.title: The Baganda: An Account of Their Native Customs and Folklore
source_references.author: Roscoe, John

title: Kibuka: The War God of Buganda
summary: Kibuka is the fearsome war god of the Baganda people, the brother of the lake god Mukasa. According to legend, Kibuka was once a mortal man of extraordinary strength and military genius. When Buganda was threatened by invasion from the neighboring Bunyoro kingdom, Kibuka was called upon to lead the army. He fought with supernatural ferocity, invisible to his enemies, raining arrows down upon them from a cloud. The enemy could not see him but could feel his arrows, and they fled in terror. However, Kibuka grew overconfident. While resting in a fig tree, he boasted loudly about his invincibility. An enemy soldier heard him and shot an arrow into the tree, striking Kibuka in the chest. He fell dead, and his body was discovered by the Baganda. After his death, Kibuka was deified and his shrine at Butikkiro became a national sanctuary where kings prayed before battle. Kibuka's story teaches that even the greatest warrior can fall to pride and carelessness.
source: Ganda oral tradition, Buganda kingdom folklore
moral: Overconfidence is the deadliest enemy of even the greatest warrior.

source_references.title: The Holy Lake of the Baganda
source_references.author: Roscoe, John
source_references.publisher: In: The Journal of the Royal Anthropological Institute, Vol. 34, pp. 349–372

title: Kaang's Creation: The Great Tree and the First People
summary: The San creator god Kaang (also known as /Kaggen) created a wondrous tree whose branches stretched over the entire land. At its base he dug a hole reaching down to the underworld where people and animals lived in harmony. He led them up into the world, instructing them not to build fires. When the sun set, the people grew cold and afraid, disobeying Kaang's command. The fire frightened the animals, who fled to caves and mountains, and from that day forward humans lost the ability to communicate with animals. This myth explains the separation between humans and nature and the origin of animal wildness.

source_references.title: The Early Bushman Way of Life and Its Continuity
source_references.author: Lee, Richard B.
source_references.publisher: In: The Past of the Hunter-Gatherers, ed. C. Schrire, Prentice-Hall, pp. 176–200

title: The Kano Chronicle and the Founding of the Seven Hausa States
summary: The Kano Chronicle records the oral traditions of the Hausa people, tracing the founding of the seven legitimate Hausa states (Hausa Bakwai) to the sons of Bayajidda. After Bayajidda married Queen Daurama of Daura, their son Bawo fathered six sons who founded Kano, Katsina, Zaria, Gobir, Rano, and Daura itself, while Bayajidda's elder son Biram founded the seventh state. Kano grew into a major commercial hub of trans-Saharan trade, Katsina became a center of Islamic learning, and Zaria was later ruled by the legendary warrior queen Amina. This genealogy serves as a political charter that structured hierarchy, succession, and identity across Hausaland for centuries.

title: Kikala: The First Fulani Shepherd and the Sacred Cattle
summary: According to Fulani legend, the first Fulani shepherd was Kikala, whose hand with six fingers contains the primordial genealogy of the Fulani people. Four fingers represent the four great Fulani aristocratic families (Dyal, Ba, So, and Bari), the fifth finger represents allied tribes, and the sixth represents those in symbiosis with the Fulani. Shortly before his death, Kikala divided his cattle among his first four sons by color, establishing the clan system. The Fulani believe cattle originated from water, and the annual Lotori ceremony drives herds to waterholes in remembrance of their watery origin. The number four governs Fulani cosmology: four elements, four cardinal directions, and four basic cattle hide colors.

title: Koumen: The Fulani Initiation and the Mysteries of Pastoral Life
summary: In Fulani esoteric tradition, Koumen is the shepherd of the primordial cattle and the depositary of pastoral initiation secrets. He was entrusted by Gueno, the eternal creator, with the care of the land and all animals. Koumen can transform wild animals into domestic ones and vice versa, appearing to humans as a child between three and eleven years old. His wife Foroforodu assists him in transmitting secret knowledge. Those who complete the cycle of initiation gain the ability to understand animal language and become Silatigi, masters of ritual speech. The initiate learns that the universe was formed 'from a drop of milk' containing the four elements, which formed the 'hermaphrodite bovine,' symbol of all creation. The cow occupies the central place as the first creature, forming a mythical triad with woman and the universe.

source_references.title: The Fulani of the Niger Delta: A Study of Their Changing Social World
source_references.author: Nurse, Andrew T.

title: Kupe Discovering Aotearoa
summary: Kupe, a great Polynesian navigator and chief from Hawaiki, pursued the giant octopus Te Wheke-a-Muturangi across the Pacific Ocean after it stole his bait and caused chaos. His wife Hine-te-Aparangi sighted land and named it Aotearoa (Land of the Long White Cloud). Kupe and his crew landed at the Hokianga Harbour, where he battled and killed the giant octopus. He explored the coastline, named many landmarks, and returned to Hawaiki with tales of the new land, providing the first detailed navigational knowledge that later guided the great migration of Maori to New Zealand.

title: The Kumulipo: Hawaiian Creation Chant
summary: The Kumulipo is the sacred Hawaiian creation chant, a genealogical epic tracing the universe from its primordial origins. It begins in total darkness (Po) with the birth of coral, seaweed, and the first sea creatures. The chant follows the emergence of life from ocean to land, from the humblest plants to animals and finally humans. The chant connects the Hawaiian chiefly lineage directly to the gods, establishing mana through unbroken descent from the primal darkness. The Kumulipo was famously chanted in full at the birth of Kamehameha the Great, and later translated by Queen Liliuokalani. It stands as one of the greatest surviving works of Polynesian oral literature.

title: Kamata the Culture Hero of Vanuatu
summary: Kamata is a culture hero in Vanuatu mythology, particularly among the people of Ambrym and Malekula. He is credited with bringing fire, agriculture, and essential knowledge to humanity. Kamata journeyed across the islands performing great deeds, shaping the landscape, and teaching people how to cultivate yams, build houses, and conduct proper ceremonies. He is associated with the volcano spirits of Ambrym, a region known for its active volcanic activity. Kamata's exploits often involve tricking dangerous spirits and defeating monstrous beings. His stories are part of the larger cycle of Melanesian culture heroes who brought civilization to humanity and established social customs that continue to be observed.

title: The Kakamora: Elves of the Solomon Islands
summary: The Kakamora are mythical elf-like creatures in the folklore of Makira and Guadalcanal in the Solomon Islands. They are described as small, hairy beings with great strength, living in the deep forests and remote mountains. The Kakamora are known to be shy but mischievous, sometimes stealing food from villages or playing tricks on travelers. In some traditions they are considered forest guardians who protect sacred groves and punish those who disrespect nature. Their small size allows them to move through the forest undetected. Stories about the Kakamora serve as cautionary tales, warning children and adults alike to respect the boundaries between human settlements and the wild spirit-inhabited forests of the Solomon Islands.

title: Kahausibware: The Creator Goddess
summary: Kahausibware is a creator goddess in the mythology of the Mono-Alu people of the Solomon Islands. She is a supreme female deity credited with creating the world, the islands, and all living things. In the beginning, Kahausibware created the land by pulling islands up from the sea. She then fashioned humans from clay and breathed life into them, teaching them how to build canoes, plant gardens, and fish. As a creator goddess, she represents the feminine creative principle and is associated with fertility, growth, and the abundance of the sea and forest. Her worship was central to Mono-Alu religious life, with offerings made to ensure good harvests and successful fishing expeditions.

title: Kilibob and Manup: The Two Brothers of Papua New Guinea
summary: In the mythology of northeastern Papua New Guinea, Kilibob and Manup are mythical brothers whose travels created the islands and peoples of the region. Kilibob traveled by canoe along the northern coast, creating islands and bestowing gifts like fishing nets, canoes, and knowledge of navigation. Manup followed a southern route and brought agriculture, domesticated animals, and ceremonial practices. Wherever they landed, the brothers left behind different languages and customs. Their journeys explain the cultural diversity of Papua New Guinea's coastal peoples. The brothers eventually became rivals, and their conflict is said to have created the distinct identities of the different language groups, as each brother favored different communities with different skills and knowledge.

title: Korkut Ata and the Search for Immortality
summary: Korkut Ata (also Dede Korkut) is the legendary father of Kazakh music and the creator of the kobyz (horsehair string instrument). Unable to accept the inevitability of death, Korkut fled from human society into nature, seeking immortality. But every element of nature — the trees, mountains, steppes, and rivers — told him that they too were subject to decay and death. In his solitary anguish, Korkut hollowed out a tree called shirgay to make the first kobyz, pulled horsehair strings across it, and began to play. The music was so transcendent that it brought him immortality through art. His melodies still echo across the Kazakh steppe, and his burial place on the Syr Darya riverbank is a pilgrimage site.

title: The Kazan Creation Myth
summary: The founding myth of Kazan explains how the city got its name. According to legend, a Bulgar prince was hunting on the banks of the Volga when his party stopped to prepare a meal. They put a copper cauldron (kazan in Tatar) on the fire to boil water. A serpent emerged from the water, warning the prince that a great city would rise on this spot but would face many trials. Frightened, the prince's men fled, leaving the cauldron behind. When they returned, the cauldron had filled with gold coins. The prince interpreted this as a sign from Tengri and founded the city of Kazan on the site. Another version connects the city's foundation to Queen Söyembikä, who threw a golden cauldron into the river while fleeing enemies, marking the spot where the city would be built.

title: Kyys Debiliye (The Warrior Maiden)
summary: A rare Yakut Olonkho epic featuring a female warrior as its main protagonist. Kyys Debiliye is a powerful heroine who fights alongside and against male heroes, demonstrating the full range of martial prowess and determination. She defends her people against the evil Abaasy spirits and enemies that threaten the Middle World. The epic is notable for its strong female central character in a traditionally male-dominated genre and reflects the high status women could hold in Yakut mythology. It was published in the series 'Monuments of Folklore of the Peoples of Siberia and the Far East'.

title: Kuutkh (Raven) Creates the World
summary: The central creation myth of the Chukchi, Koryak, and Itelmen peoples of northeastern Siberia. Kuutkh, the Great Raven, is both creator and trickster who shaped the cosmos from primordial chaos. In the beginning, there was nothing but darkness and water. Kuutkh flew over the endless sea and, through his actions — purposeful and accidental — created land, mountains, rivers, and valleys. He brought light to the world, taught humans essential survival skills including fire-making, hunting techniques, and net-weaving. Kuutkh can shift between raven and human form and his unpredictable nature, blending divine intention with mischievous impulse, mirrors the volatility of the Arctic environment itself.

title: Khan Töölö the Hero
summary: The Tuvan epic of Khan Töölö, one of the most important figures in Tuvan oral tradition. The epic tells of a heroic warrior-prince who defends the Tuvan people against invading forces and monstrous beings from the Sayan and Altai mountains. Khan Töölö possesses supernatural strength granted by the sky spirits and rides a magical horse that can speak and fly. The epic is traditionally performed with throat singing accompaniment, with different vocal styles representing different characters — deep kargyraa for the hero, high sygyt for maidens, and rhythmic khoomei for battle scenes. The story reinforces Tuvan values of bravery, hospitality, respect for nature, and the sacred bond between a hero and his horse. The epic can take many hours to perform in its complete form.

title: Khun Borom: The Founding Myth of the Lao People
summary: The Khun Borom myth is the foundational creation story of the Lao and Tai peoples, detailing their origins after a great flood. According to the legend recorded in the Nithan Khun Borom chronicle, in ancient times humanity became wicked and corrupt. A powerful sky deity (Phaya Thaen) destroyed them with a great flood, sparing only three righteous chiefs who were preserved in heaven. The deity sent the chiefs back to earth with a sacred buffalo to help them cultivate the land. They descended at Muang Then (modern Dien Bien Phu in Vietnam). After the land was prepared for rice cultivation, the buffalo died, and from its nostril grew a bitter gourd vine. From the gourds emerged the new human race — darker-skinned peoples from gourds cut with a hot skewer, and lighter-skinned Tai peoples from gourds cut with a chisel. The gods taught the people to build houses, cultivate rice, and observe proper rituals. As the population grew, Phaya Thaen sent his son Khun Borom to rule as the first king of the Tai people. After 25 years, Khun Borom divided his kingdom among his seven sons, who became the founders of the major Tai kingdoms across Southeast Asia, including Lan Xang (Laos), Lanna (northern Thailand), Ayutthaya, and others.
moral: Civilization is a gift from the divine, and kingship is a sacred trust to guide humanity toward righteousness.
source: Lao chronicles (Nithan Khun Borom), Tai oral tradition

title: Krishna Tames the Serpent Kaliya
summary: Young Krishna, during his childhood in Vrindavan, discovered that the Yamuna River had become poisoned by Kaliya, a massive multi-headed serpent who had taken residence in its waters. The river boiled with venom, killing fish, birds, and trees along the banks, and the villagers could no longer drink or approach it. Without fear, Krishna dove into the deepest part of the river. The serpent wrapped him in his coils, but Krishna expanded his body, broke free, and began dancing on Kaliya's thousand hoods. As Krishna danced, each hood was crushed under his divine feet. The serpent, bleeding and exhausted, surrendered. His wives prayed for mercy, and Krishna spared Kaliya's life but banished him and his family to the ocean, restoring the Yamuna to purity.
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: Kannagi and the Burning of Madurai (Silappadikaram)
summary: Kannagi, a virtuous merchant's daughter, married Kovalan in the Chola city of Puhar. When Kovalan fell for the courtesan Madhavi and squandered all his wealth, he eventually returned to Kannagi. The couple traveled to Madurai, where Kovalan tried to sell one of Kannagi's jeweled anklets to restart his life. The royal goldsmith, who had stolen the queen's similar anklet, falsely accused Kovalan of theft. The Pandya king ordered Kovalan's execution without proper trial. Kannagi stormed into court, broke open her anklet to reveal rubies (the queen's held pearls), proving her husband's innocence. The king died of shame, but Kannagi, in divine fury, tore off her breast and hurled it at the kingdom, cursing Madurai to burn to ashes — a curse that consumed the entire city.
source: Silappadikaram (The Jeweled Anklet), by Ilango Adigal (c. 5th-6th century CE)
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: Kuveni: The Yakshini Queen
summary: Kuveni (also known as Sesapati or Kuvanna) was a Yakshini queen ruling in Lanka when Prince Vijaya arrived. Described as a shape-shifting demoness who could assume the form of a beautiful sixteen-year-old maiden, she initially imprisoned Vijaya's men in a chasm. After Vijaya overpowered her, she swore loyalty and helped him conquer the Yakkha city of Sirisavatthu. She bore him two children, Jivahatta and Disala. Vijaya later repudiated her saying 'men fear superhuman beings' and sent her away. When Kuveni returned to her own people, they killed her as a traitor. The Vedda people venerate her as Maha Loku Kiriammaleththo.
moral: True honor lives on in stories

title: King Dutugemunu and Elara: The Just Rivalry
culture: Sri Lankan Sinhalese / Sri Lankan Tamil
summary: Elara (Ellalan), a Tamil Chola prince, ruled Anuradhapura with such famed justice that he earned the title Manu Needhi Cholan ('the Chola who follows justice') — he once executed his own son under a chariot wheel to give justice to a grieving cow. The young Sinhalese prince Dutugemunu, believing the island should be united under Buddhism, waged a campaign against the aging Elara. Their climactic battle took place at the south gate of Anuradhapura, where Dutugemunu killed Elara in single combat atop their war elephants (Kandula vs Maha Pabbatha). Despite victory, Dutugemunu honored his foe with royal cremation and a monument, decreeing that passing princes must silence their music out of respect.
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: Koimala: The First King of the Maldives
summary: According to Maldivian folklore, Prince Koimala was a prince from the Indian subcontinent (or Sri Lanka, in some versions) who sailed to the Maldives with two ships. The indigenous Giraavaru people — Dravidian fishermen who cleaned tuna on a large sandbank — spotted his vessel and welcomed him, allowing him to settle on the blood-tainted sandbank. Trees were planted there (the first being a papaya tree) and the island was named Maa-le (Malé, meaning 'big blood'). The islanders, impressed by his royal lineage, proclaimed him king. He unified the Maldives from Minicoy to Addu, establishing Malé as the seat of power. Koimala founded the House of Theemuge and the Lunar Dynasty. While some versions identify him as Dharumavantha Rasgefaanu (the first Muslim ruler), historical records show his successor Dhovemi actually converted to Islam in 1153. The Giraavaru are considered the original inhabitants of the Maldives.
source: Maldivian oral tradition, Koimala Qaumee album, Isdhoo Loamaafaanu
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: King Solomon and the Jinn
summary: According to Islamic and Arabic folklore, Prophet Solomon (Sulayman) was granted dominion over all beings: mankind, jinn, birds, wild beasts, and the wind. He commanded armies of jinn and devils, imprisoned rebellious marids and ifrits in brass bottles sealed with molten lead and his signet ring, and cast them into the seas. He understood the speech of birds and animals, and his throne was carried by the wind. His vizier Asaf possessed the knowledge to bring the Queen of Sheba's throne instantly. Solomon's ring, bearing the Most Great Name of God, was the source of his power, and when lost to a demon named Sakhr, his kingdom was briefly usurped.

title: Kaveh the Blacksmith and the Derafsh Kaviani
summary: Kaveh, a humble blacksmith, loses all but one of his sons to the daily sacrifice demanded by King Zahhak's serpent-shoulders. He storms the royal court, refusing the king's bribe of signing his remaining son's death warrant. Kaveh raises his blacksmith's leather apron on a spear as a battle standard and leads the populace in revolt. He seeks out Feraydun, the rightful heir, and guides him to overthrow Zahhak. The leather apron, embellished with jewels over centuries, became the Derafsh Kaviani, the legendary imperial standard of Iran used until the Arab conquest. Kaveh symbolizes righteous popular rebellion against tyranny.

title: Khosrow and Shirin
summary: The Persian prince Khosrow Parviz falls in love with the Armenian princess Shirin through a portrait painted by his friend Shapur. Their love is thwarted by a series of obstacles: Khosrow's forced marriage to the Byzantine princess Mariam for political alliance, his dalliance with Shekar in Isfahan, and the tragic love of the sculptor Farhad who carves Mount Behistun for Shirin's sake (and dies upon hearing false news of her death). After Mariam's death, Khosrow finally marries Shirin. But Khosrow's son Shiroyeh, who also desires Shirin, murders his father. Shirin, to avoid marrying Shiroyeh, takes poison and dies on her husband's body. They are buried together.

title: Kawa the Blacksmith and Newroz
summary: The foundational Kurdish myth explaining the origins of Newroz (the Kurdish New Year on the spring equinox). The evil Assyrian king Zahhak (Zuhak) had serpents growing from his shoulders that demanded daily sacrifices of young men's brains. Kawa, a blacksmith who lost all but one of his sons to the sacrifice, rallied the people in revolt. He led them to the mountains, forged weapons, and stormed Zahhak's palace, killing him with his blacksmith's hammer. Kawa then lit a bonfire on the mountaintop to signal victory — a fire that spread across the land, melting winter and heralding spring. The Derafsh Kaviani (Kawa's leather apron on a spear) became the symbol of the uprising. Today Kurds celebrate Newroz by lighting bonfires and jumping over flames.
